patient_history.chief_complaint: rapidly enlarging pulsatile mass on medial right upper extremity in a 41-day-old infant

input.patient_transcript:
Doctor: Hi, how are you today?
Parent: I'm good, thanks.
Doctor: Great. Can you tell me what brought you here today?
Parent: My baby is here for evaluation of a rapidly enlarging mass on his right arm.
Doctor: I see. Can you tell me a bit more about your baby?
Parent: He's a 41-day-old male infant with hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy.
Doctor: And when was the mass first noticed?
Parent: It was about 5 hours ago.
Doctor: And has it been rapidly enlarging since then?
Parent: Yes, it has been getting bigger.
Doctor: Has your baby had any previous medical procedures on that arm?
Parent: Yes, he had a PICC line placed there during the neonatal period for treatment of Lennox-Gastaut syndrome without status epilepticus.
Doctor: Understood. What did you notice on the physical examination?
Parent: The mass was pulsatile, but his capillary refill was within 2 seconds, pulses were good in all extremities, his arms and legs were warm, and there was no discoloration or swelling.
Doctor: We obtained a CT angiogram, which showed a pseudoaneurysm of the right distal brachial artery measuring about 1.8 by 1.6 by 2.1 cm.
Parent: Okay.
Doctor: An MRI then showed a 2.1 by 1.7 by 1.1 cm mass along the right brachial artery with vascular flow, consistent with a pseudoaneurysm.
Parent: I see.
Doctor: Because of concern for a vascular abnormality related to the prior line, we admitted him to the neonatal intensive care unit for observation. Over the next two days the mass continued to grow, and plastic surgery and vascular surgery were consulted. Your baby is scheduled for microscope-assisted brachial artery repair with both teams involved.
Parent: Thank you for explaining.
input.chart_notes: 39-week term, 41-day-old male infant with hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy presented to the ED with a rapidly enlarging, pulsatile mass on the medial aspect of the right upper extremity. Parents first noticed the mass 5 hours before arrival; it had enlarged rapidly. Neurovascular exam showed a warm, well-perfused limb with 2+ brachial, radial, and ulnar pulses, capillary refill <2 seconds, no discoloration or edema, and age-appropriate movement of all extremities. Past history notable for PICC placement in the same region during the neonatal period for treatment of Lennox-Gastaut syndrome without status epilepticus. CT angiography demonstrated a pseudoaneurysm of the right distal brachial artery measuring approximately 1.8 × 1.6 × 2.1 cm with clear communication to the brachial artery lumen and no other vascular abnormalities. Contrast-enhanced MRI confirmed a 2.1 × 1.7 × 1.1 cm mass along the right brachial artery with vascular flow. The infant was admitted to the NICU for observation; over 2 days the mass continued to grow, and plastic and vascular surgery teams were consulted. Under general anesthesia, the pseudoaneurysm was exposed via curvilinear incision at the elbow, the diseased segment resected, and a tension-free end-to-end microvascular anastomosis of the brachial artery (2 mm diameter) was performed with 9-0 nylon. Postoperatively, the infant received low-dose intravenous heparin (10 U/kg/h) for 48 hours, had warm, well-perfused upper extremities without bleeding, and was discharged on postoperative day 3 with an arm splint and oral acetaminophen suspension for pain. At 2-year follow-up, the surgical site was well healed, brachial/ulnar/radial pulses were symmetric, and right upper extremity function was normal.

prediction.clinical_summary: A 41-day-old term male infant with hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy and Lennox-Gastaut syndrome presented with a rapidly enlarging pulsatile mass of the medial right upper extremity, first noticed 5 hours before presentation. Neurovascular examination remained intact, and CTA/MRI demonstrated a right distal brachial artery pseudoaneurysm likely related to prior PICC placement in the same region. The lesion enlarged during NICU observation, prompting surgical resection and microscope-assisted end-to-end brachial artery repair, with good postoperative recovery and normal function at long-term follow-up.

differential_diagnosis.condition: Right distal brachial artery pseudoaneurysm secondary to prior PICC line trauma
differential_diagnosis.rationale: Most likely given the rapidly enlarging pulsatile mass, direct imaging evidence of communication with the brachial artery lumen, vascular flow on MRI, and history of prior PICC placement in the same arm.

A pulsatile upper extremity mass with arterial flow could represent a true aneurysm, but this is less likely because imaging specifically described a pseudoaneurysm and the history suggests iatrogenic arterial wall injury.

Could present as an enlarging vascular soft-tissue mass in an infant, but is less likely because the lesion developed acutely over hours and imaging showed a focal arterial outpouching communicating with the brachial artery rather than a congenital vascular lesion.

final_report.subjective: 41-day-old term male infant presented with a rapidly enlarging pulsatile mass on the medial right upper extremity, first noticed by parents about 5 hours prior to ED presentation. Past history is significant for hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy and Lennox-Gastaut syndrome without status epilepticus. Parent reports prior PICC line placement in the same arm during the neonatal period. No reported discoloration or swelling of the limb.
final_report.objective: Exam demonstrated a pulsatile right upper extremity mass with preserved perfusion: warm extremities, capillary refill less than 2 seconds, 2+ brachial/radial/ulnar pulses, no edema or discoloration, and age-appropriate movement of all extremities. CT angiography showed a pseudoaneurysm of the right distal brachial artery measuring approximately 1.8 x 1.6 x 2.1 cm with communication to the brachial artery lumen and no other vascular abnormalities. Contrast-enhanced MRI confirmed a 2.1 x 1.7 x 1.1 cm mass along the right brachial artery with vascular flow. During NICU observation over 2 days, the mass continued to enlarge. The infant underwent operative exposure and resection of the diseased arterial segment with tension-free end-to-end microvascular brachial artery anastomosis under general anesthesia. Postoperatively, he received low-dose IV heparin infusion for 48 hours, remained warm and well perfused without bleeding, and was discharged on postoperative day 3 with an arm splint and acetaminophen as needed. At 2-year follow-up, pulses were symmetric, the incision was well healed, and right upper extremity function was normal.
final_report.assessment: Right distal brachial artery pseudoaneurysm, most likely iatrogenic from prior PICC placement, presenting as a rapidly enlarging pulsatile upper extremity mass in a neonate/young infant. Neurovascular status remained intact despite interval growth, but enlargement warranted operative management. Postoperative course was uncomplicated with successful vascular reconstruction and excellent long-term outcome.
final_report.plan: Admit for close neurovascular monitoring and serial examination of the right upper extremity. Obtain/confirm vascular imaging as performed (CTA and MRI) and involve plastic surgery and vascular surgery. Proceed with microscope-assisted surgical repair consisting of pseudoaneurysm resection and primary end-to-end microvascular brachial artery anastomosis. Postoperatively, continue low-dose IV heparin for 48 hours, monitor for bleeding and distal perfusion, maintain arm protection with splinting, provide oral acetaminophen for pain control, and arrange surgical follow-up to assess wound healing, pulses, and limb function.
